Learn

Why completing the square works

Every quadratic axÂ² + bx + c hides a perfect square. The xÂ² term is a literal square of side x. The bx term is a strip of width b you can split in half and wrap around two sides of that square.

Wrapping leaves one empty corner. Fill it with a small (bâ„2)Â² square and the whole shape becomes a clean square of side (x + bâ„2). That single idea turns any quadratic into (x + bâ„2)Â² = a number â€” and from there, x is one square-root away.

It is also where the quadratic formula comes from: do this with letters instead of numbers and x = (âˆ’b Â± âˆš(bÂ² âˆ’ 4ac)) â„ 2a falls straight out.
